Common.Name: common dandelion
Collection.List: Vascular Plant Survey of a Rock Bluff System on Galiano Island2016(unpublished technical report)
Notes: Taraxacum as a genus is highly overlooked (C.R.Björk); a great diversity of Taraxacum spp. have yet to be reported for the project; PNW botanists are happy to adopt European microspecies when there are few of them (e.g. R. armeniacus: a microspecies consisting of a single clone). But there are too many species of Taraxacum in BC for this to be applied without very hard work and pain. But again there are real problems with using T. officinale and T. erythrospermum in the FPNW sense as they lump things which have different evolutionary origins. Best practice would be to use sections as broad level taxa or “species" as these can be recognized quite easily with a bit of training (for instance Galiano has Taraxacum sect. Hamatum as its commonest dandelion group). We are not there yet... Pro tem., I suggest using "T. officinale agg.", and "T. erythrospermum agg." (without authorities - they are not biologically species). There is a long road before BC botanists will accept a more biologically meaningful treatment of Taraxacum diversity. This does not much matter for the introductions (and there are no natives on Galiano), but is an absolute tragedy for the natives where a fantastic richness of endemic alpine species languish under the completely ridiculous name “T. ceratophorum”. (T. ceratophorum is an asian species that looks nothing like anything in BC!).
Collection_std: Vascular Plant Survey of a Rock Bluff System on Galiano Island2016(unpublished technical report)

Common.Name: yarrow
Notes: nominate subspecies not yet observed on project; varieties historically recognized on Galiano Island include: A.m. borealis (239064), A.m. californica (239065), & A.m. lanulosa (239065); according to provincial authorities, our native yarrows are now lumped into A. borealis following Ramsey et al. (2008); A. millefolium is recognized as an exotic species in the BC Flora; David Giblin, gives this account for why A.millefolium is adopted in FPNW2: "One of the problems that I have with the Achillea situation is that it takes us down the path of assigning different names to similar looking plants of different ploidy levels.That is not a functional system for field botanists because there are often no clear morphological markers among populations with different ploidy levels.This issue also applies to Campanula rotundifolia, another widespread species with everything from diploid to beyond octoploid populations.Perhaps had Justin provided a key to distinguish A. millefolium from A. borealis he would have had better success advancing his argument.I think that it is highly unlikely that no A. millefolium plants of Eurasian origin have been introduced into western North America.Without a key, it is safest to call everything A. millefolium."

Notes: Taraxacum as a genus is highly overlooked (C.R.Björk); a great diversity of Taraxacum spp. have yet to be reported for the project; PNW botanists are happy to adopt European microspecies when there are few of them (e.g. R. armeniacus: a microspecies consisting of a single clone). But there are too many species of Taraxacum in BC for this to be applied without very hard work and pain. But again there are real problems with using T. officinale and T. erythrospermum in the FPNW sense as they lump things which have different evolutionary origins. Best practice would be to use sections as broad level taxa or “species" as these can be recognized quite easily with a bit of training (for instance Galiano has Taraxacum sect. Hamatum as its commonest dandelion group). We are not there yet... Pro tem., I suggest using "T. officinale agg.", and "T. erythrospermum agg." (without authorities - they are not biologically species). There is a long road before BC botanists will accept a more biologically meaningful treatment of Taraxacum diversity. This does not much matter for the introductions (and there are no natives on Galiano), but is an absolute tragedy for the natives where a fantastic richness of endemic alpine species languish under the completely ridiculous name “T. ceratophorum”. (T. ceratophorum is an asian species that looks nothing like anything in BC!).
